title: Executive Order 11803—Establishing a Clemency Board to Review Certain Convictions of Persons Under Section 12 or 6 (j) of the Military Selective Service Act and Certain Discharges Issued Because of, and Certain Convictions for, Violations of Article 85, 86, or 87 of the Uniform Code of Military Justice and to Make Recommendations for Executive Clemency with Respect Thereto

flags.justification: The Board is purely advisory to the President -- Article II clemency power is already constitutionally vested solely in the President regardless of the Board's existence; the Board adds no new concentration beyond that pre-existing constitutional baseline.

No described public transparency mechanism for individual case outcomes, no specified term length or removal standard for the 9 presidentially-appointed members, and no conflict-of-interest safeguards described in the order's text.
Single coherent purpose despite covering both Selective Service Act violations and UCMJ desertion-adjacent discharges.
Eligibility precisely bounded: specific USC/UCMJ sections, specific violation types (enumerated i-v), specific date range (Aug. 4, 1964 - Mar. 28, 1973).

Sec. 9's explicit, self-executing termination clause -- 'shall submit its final recommendations... not later than December 31, 1976, at which time it shall cease to exist' -- a genuine, clean sunset.

One of the most historically significant orders of the Ford presidency -- the operational implementation of Ford's September 16, 1974 conditional-amnesty Proclamation for Vietnam-era draft evaders and military deserters, affecting many thousands of individual cases, issued just five weeks after Nixon's resignation and one week after Ford's controversial pardon of Nixon (a Proclamation, not captured in this EO corpus).
No inter-agency conflict; GSA provides administrative support on a reimbursable basis (Sec. 7).
No exemption architecture as such; eligibility criteria are inclusion criteria, not exemptions from a generally-applicable rule.

notes: One of this batch's most historically significant orders -- establishes the Presidential Clemency Board implementing Ford's Vietnam-era conditional-amnesty program, with a genuine, clean Dec. 31, 1976 self-executing sunset (Sec. 9) but a real accountability gap: no described public transparency, term limits, or removal standard for the 9 presidentially-appointed members reviewing what would become roughly 15,000 individual clemency cases.
